title: ADVANCED BOTOX ANATOMY: How to avoid nasty lower-face side effects [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: ADVANCED BOTOX ANATOMY： How to avoid nasty lower-face side effects [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: If you over treat them or if you treat them accidentally or you treat them asymmetrically you're going to get a side effect which is extremely noticeable welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about lower face muscles …

segments.text: if you over treat them or if you treat them accidentally or you treat them asymmetrically you're going to get a side effect which is extremely noticeable welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about lower face muscles very important for advanced botox we're going to cover some key concepts the muscles you need to know and several tips and tricks that i've been teaching for years so give us an overview of the lower face muscles we need to know about there's a lot to take in so prepare yourself but also if you have faith that you're going to learn something from the show please give us a like now it'll also make sure that youtube shows you future videos next time you log on first of all let's think about the face um as on a higher level so before we dive into the detailed anatomy what's actually going on in the lower face so the lower face is much more complicated than the upper face i think and obviously eyes are a very delicate structure and there's a lot of interesting anatomy there

interesting anatomy there but the actual function of the upper face is broadly speaking non-verbal communication and protection of the eyes is how i understand it so you can close your eyes tight and you can protect them but there's not actually a lot of other stuff compared to the lower face where the lower face we have mastication and we also have um speech and then non-verbal communication as well so it's doing all these things all the time well maybe not all the time but certainly at different times you're doing all of these functions so it's a lot more complex it's a lot more mobile do you think about how much movement there is in the lower face compared to the upper face um it's a in most people it's a lot more mobile so a lot many more different variations and nuances i think um but it all starts really with understanding the anatomy of the muscles if you're going to understand the movement so talk us through the layout of the lower face muscles so there's some interesting differences with the muscles in the lower face compared with other muscles in your body um if you think about what the average muscle is doing is it's connecting bone to bone so

to bone so most of the other muscles when you contract them there's an insertion in an origin point which tends to be bone to bone what's different about the face in particular is that you have a lot of muscles connected either to other muscles or into the skin and that's because there's so much movement required and that depends which part you look at i'm talking mainly in this case with the anterior face so the anterior face all those muscles that control the mouth and elevate the lip and uh and there and even move the chin are all skin to bone or bone to skin depending on the way you're looking at it um muscles domestication are a bit different they are the classic bone to bone connections and you don't see those connections in the surface of the skin so that's the first general layout now when we cover the fat pads which is another video you should have a look at um that this is why there's this interesting layout where the muscle and the fat pads are like tiles on a roof where they're stacked together in the deep fat pads are actually on top of other muscles so yeah check that video out and they'll tell you more about that but that's the first thing that's different about the muscles in the face is that they don't have that classic

connections that the rest of the skeletal muscle does the next thing is to think about like how are they actually supporting and holding the structures and i kind of think about a lot of the mid-face being basically hanging off the zygoma and the maxilla so all those bone bony attachments are either on the maxilla or the zygoma and they're hanging that the mouth the nose um the chin is all supported on those muscles anteriorly and i do kind of almost imagine it being sort of hanging there supporting but there are obviously elevators and depressors which we'll look at in detail so tell us about the elevators so these are the muscles that lift the mouth almost directly up basically um the zygomatic major for example is sometimes not included on the diagrams but it's also an elevator it's just more pulling the corner of the mouth rather than pulling directly upwards the core elevators we need to know are the levator labia electro nasi muscle medially then there's the levator labii superioris the levator anguli oris and zygomatic

minor and those muscles are all doing very many interesting uh different expressions obviously they're involved slightly in smiling they're also involved in some looks of disgust for example um they're definitely involved in the really big expressions if you're smiling a lot and the time when we treat them is usually with gummy smile if you're treating either with toxin or dermal fillers if you're trying to moderate the degree of movement in that area it's those muscles that you're trying to dampen or reduce their strength so are there any side effects we need to worry about with the elevators so yes absolutely if you over treat them or if you treat them accidentally or you treat them asymmetrically you're going to get a side effect which is extremely noticeable because if you cause any problem with a patient's smile it's very obviously obvious to their friends and family and to them and obviously your smile is a very important thing so an asymmetrical smile is a fairly devastating side effect of treating a little bit too much on one side or the other so classically this happens when people inject the orbicularis oculi muscle a

little bit too deeply go all the way through the fat pad and hit the origin of the zygomatic major so that would be a really awful side effect where you get an asymmetrical smile similarly with gummy smile treatments if you're trying to relax the levator labial nasal muscle then if you get that asymmetrically you might get one side that lifts and the other that doesn't and much more subtly than that is a slightly odd smile now the interesting thing about gummy smile is you can often get a brilliant before and after picture that looks amazing for you know for showing clients what's possible but the patient themselves really doesn't like it and it's because of the dynamic nature of a smile that if you smile that you're suppressing the elevator it can to their people who know their faces really well it can just look either insincere or a bit odd and over the years you know talking over 12 years of a handful of patients who haven't liked it because every time they smile people know them well say what are you being sarcastic or you're not quite you know because it just doesn't seem like their

normal smile so it's worth building into the consultation that even if you get a technically brilliant result that it may look it may take some getting used to let me put it that way and in a percentage of patients it may not be worth the benefit that they get and you might want to try a different method for example using dermal filler so that sounds a bit tricky is there anything that we can do to prevent it sounds like if we do our best that might still happen with a gummy smile yeah well that one in particular is tricky because it's more of a familiarity type experience so it's when and there are many patients who are very happy with the result themselves and they don't care what their friends and family think and there are others who don't like the every time they smile people saying not getting them so you need to build it into the consultation and the other thing is i've over the years reduced my dose right down so i only use for a first pass on a new patient maybe one unit each side um whereas some people would use four units and that's only because i would rather just top them up like i said last week i like to take the approach of it being a journey rather

than a destination new patients i'm more than happy to treat them with a low dose and see them again one more time to get them happy without any downside and once you know them you can up the dose [Music] or at least keep going with the dose you know is safe so the side effects quite scary where you'd be treating crow's feet and end up affecting the smile are there any other treatments that might affect the smile well yes actually there's another actually relatively common i think for the percentage of times the treatment is done is um is when you're treating the master if you get a little bit superior and a little bit medial then you can affect the resource muscle and that causes sometimes an even more upsetting asymmetry of smiling and that that's basically because you're relaxing a little muscle that pulls the corner of the mouth uh when you do a big smile so what can we do to avoid that so yeah one of the problems i realize with this is if you google a master reduction botox pattern or injection points for toxin you get a very common description that if you actually look at the lines

which i'm sure we get up on the screen for you you'll see that the line that is often used draws a line right to the oral commissure and if you look at the top kind of right hand side of where that line is inferior to it you're actually very close to the insertion point of resorius and i know from training clinicians that when you see them in practice trying to implement these things it's only half a centimeter to centimeter different and you're right in the resorius so i don't like using that line in fact i use a different line which is to really line up with the mental crease so you're treating the lower third of the master muscle but my boundary is the mental crease and that takes you away from that insertion point of resorius which i think is likely to reduce the number of side effects of this smile of the smile being affected with a toxin treatment in master so when you're injecting the masseter what do you do to reduce that risk apart from using a different boundary which means i'm less likely to inject near that insertion point of resorius the other thing that's really important when injecting the master to avoid that particular side effect as well as another side effect

another side effect is to make sure that you're deep enough so if the actual resource muscle is inserted into the fascia around the master so it's relatively superficial um and so if we make sure we're deep that's the second thing you can do so we need to be inferior to it and too deep to actually hit the risorius is an effective way of reducing that risk you said another side effect oh yes so there's a an odd side effect that happens particularly with people with really big masters and i've had a couple of these referred to me over the years where they've actually had a really good result initially often with really enormous master muscles and then a few weeks later after it's kind of kicking in they get a bump that forms on the surface of the master and really what's happening is you've got the deeper layers of the master actually herniate through the atrophied muscle at the top thankfully that the ones i've seen have been resolved quite quickly with just an additional injection but it's another reason to be deep so in order to inject more deeply you can

either compress the muscle when you're injecting or better still is to use longer needles particularly in patients with bigger muscles because the bigger the muscle is the more chance you'll be in the more superficial part of the fiber and the more chance you'll get these side effects so injecting the master is sometimes called jawline slimming but effectively you're atrophying the muscle to create more feminine often it's a more feminine shaped face but if you're masculine but you're wider and lower than your cheeks and then you might also want to do it and it's not feminizing it's just balancing so if you haven't already watched my interview with dr ruth brady it's worth checking out she goes into some extremely interesting detail on what actually could be causing masseter hypertrophy and it's to do with malocclusion and there may be many dental referrals that actually need to come off the back of these aesthetic consultations if you're going to get a complete solution to the problem because the theory is in summary and this is a very brief summary of a complex problem is that muller collusion causes biting particularly during the night it's one of the causes of bruxism

is that just that sensation of your teeth touching if you have a slight mullet occlusion problem can cause you to bite quite often and that causes hypertrophy so sometimes there's a dental problem that needs to be solved ahead of the aesthetic problem which is how patients present so worth learning about any other tips for injecting the masseter so don't just inject it because someone is wider lower down i've met a few patients who've had treatments like that and obviously they fail because there's many reasons why you can be wider lower down other than your master it's usually a fat pad sometimes it's the shape of your jaw um but similarly don't assume that it's the fat pad because i've also had some really rewarding treatments done on younger girls with big masters who think that they're fat and in examining them you can feel that they've actually got this big muscle and then treating them is a very fast process to slim them down and had some really amazing results that made them really happy so feeling is the key get them to bite down feel the shape and size of the muscle and with experience you'll start to spot it even before you examine it

has a different shape to that so we talked about elevators what about depressors so yeah the muscles that pull the corner and the midpoint of the mouth down are important to understand we actually treat them sometimes and sometimes they get treated by accident and they call side effects um so the first one to know about is the depressor angular saurus and this pulls down the corner of the mouth as you can tell by the name and that's the one that you can often treat just slightly weaken and it can elevate the corner of the mouth um as an alternative to supporting that with filler or someone who just has a dominant muscle in that area so how would you diagnose whether or not they need botox or fillers so you're looking for a hypodynamic element so during expressing if you see someone who's kind of pulling down i think like robert de niro is a bit like this he's got that slight downward pull obviously he's getting on a bit so some of its age but there's a definite hypertonic uh component to what pulled him down sherry blair has something similar but it's more of her depressor labiai so that pulls this more um directly inferior

down rather than down and lateral um so just start to look at people's faces and see what they're like during movement and you're just aiming to add a bit of harmony to the right people and it's a small dose and but i wouldn't necessarily use it to treat everyone who has a down-to-mouth because a lot of them it's fat loss and it's aging and you might want to be thinking more holistically with dermal fillers as well as your other treatment options including toxin so tell us about that side effect that we might cause on the downturn mouth without meaning to so yeah so the downturn mouth treatment it's a bit like what happens with gummy smile in that it might look okay to people who don't know you but the people who do know you can change the character of your smile if you think about what's happening if you're treating the depresses and you're not treating the elevators when you smile you might be showing a lot less of your teeth lower down and you get a smaller smile and it also a smile that pulls up more so the actual position of your mouth changes proportionately more than if you have the balancing force of the depressors fully active which is

what they are in the untreated face so it just looks different it can sometimes look a bit jokery so you get too much elevation laterally and immediately not so much and so it curves up too much these are all very subtle things that people i wouldn't say that each treatment you do you're likely to see it but occasionally patients who study themselves or they get a bit of feedback from that they'll realize that they don't like it as much as they may have casually looked at themselves because a lot of what happens with your face is quite complex the movement's complex and the people you're not even always conscious of it yourself um until it's developed you mentioned asymmetry tell us more about that well yeah the problem is it's quite common to get asymmetry because you're trying to only partially relax the muscle that always makes it harder and secondly there are these muscles are very closely overlapped so depress especially the depressor angular's auras and the depressor labiai they're and the more inferior you go the more overlapped they are and there is definitely a technique being taught where they teach you to inject along the jawline

and i think this is responsible for many um slightly bad side effects um well certainly just not the best optimal result let me put it that way and you'll see this with asymmetry and it's because you affect the depressor labia and the depressor angular saurus or both or one of them more than the other and you end up with a smaller smile when you do smile because there's nothing pulling your lower lip down if you picture a lower lip down if you picture a full smile there's an element of pulling the lower lip down and pulling the upper lip up and if you knock out one of those you get a an asymmetry to that um to that line caused by your lower lip and it's very eye-catching unfortunately so if your lips suddenly skew against your teeth people notice it immediately and so that that's uh you need to be i think there's a better technique which we can talk about and which i've been using that doesn't cause that uh the same um frequency of a problem i think what are we trying to treat when that happens well we're trying to treat depression of the mouth so it's sad face so hypertonic sometimes i do it

with belle's palsy because you get with belle's palsy because you get hypertonic muscle contractions with that and if you relax it can make a really good difference so um it can be a symmetry or it can just be a heaviness to the mouth at any age but usually it's older people and then you're just trying to relax it just enough so that the elevators pull up and you get a more pleasant aura put it that way relax it too much and you get a joke a smile and don't relax it as much on one side of the other and you get brow you get asymmetry affect the depressor labia instead of the depressor angular sorus and you get this asymmetrical lower lip and when you smile and so there are lots of things to consider i feel like i said a lot of bad things can happen most of the treatments work out well otherwise wouldn't do it at all but it's definitely complex that's why it's normally as part of an advanced toxin course so you mentioned a few minutes ago that this uh technique that you use that avoids the side effects well yeah i mean it's not a technique i've invented but it's the two versions i see taught are one is that you inject along the angle

of the jaw so you're looking for that path of depressor labiai and where it inserts you inject but unfortunately underneath that you have depressor labia so depressor angularis auras and depressor labia overlap near their insertion so you're just much more likely to affect the pressure labiai if you're injecting close to the jaw i think it's better to move up about halfway between the oral commissure and the jawline and feel for it you can actually if you ask your patient to contract you can actually feel that boundary because behind that there is a little bit of buccinator muscle uh buccinator um but that's and maybe resorious but there's not an awful lot of other stuff so if you if you're injecting um at the right point you should only and with a small dose you should hopefully only affect that depressive muscle tell me about this buccinator so i mean the vaccinated muscle is not a muscle that we ever intentionally treat but you need to be aware of it it's uh the buccal fat pad is sandwiched between the buccinator and the resource and if you're ever trying to replace volume in that area you might be using that as a guide and the only

interesting fact i know about it is that it's the strongest muscle in babies because it's what they suck with um and there's just one of the strongest muscles in a baby's body apparently when they're you know a couple months old and they're into the swing of things so it's used for sucking um but it's not really that involved with aesthetic injectables anyway and moving on down to the platysmal buns what we need to know about them so this is a very superficial muscle it's like a sheet but then it collects into these bands um and essentially you can relax it can be it's connected into the smash so if you relax it sometimes you see an improvement in jawline so it can really make a good difference and that's why it's called the nefertiti lift if you do a toxin treatment is that you create that nefertiti type jawline in a percentage of people um side effects uh similarly actually to treating the lower faces if you occasionally i've seen a few people get asymmetry in their lower mouth and it's because the muscle isn't actually as neat as it is in the textbook and it's often woven into those depresses and you

can sometimes see it you know depressor angular asaurus and the platysmal band are kind of woven together so occasionally get people get asymmetry when they smile i think it's when you're injecting very high up um but it's worth knowing about any other general tips for the lower face muscles well consultation is very important you need to be preparing your patients for the subtleties of these treatments so many of the side effects and even the results i've talked about um there are some subtleties that they need to be aware of it's not a here's your injection and you'll be happy you may there may be an adjustment period so consultation is very important and the other thing is just be precise with your doses feel the muscles i don't think you should be thinking that you need to know where the muscles are just by memory like actually feel for what you can do small amounts on your first pass and you can always add more later and then when you understand that particular when you understand that particular patient you can go up to the textbook dose and that's a good way to start because if you go too heavy on some of these more subtle treatments

more subtle treatments you'll lose patience and they won't be happy and that's obviously our reason to be is that we make our patients happy so um slowly think of it as a journey small doses feel the muscles and that should put you on a really good course to getting good results with advanced toxin so thank you to everyone who's liked this video already don't forget to like and subscribe so that you don't miss out on this great free content that we're putting out each and every week and if you would like a reference for all of the important muscles in the face for your aesthetics practice then tim has created a beautiful muscles poster and the link is down below thank you for watching take care bye
